---
title: "Linear Least Squares"
subtitle: "INFO 521 · Module 1 — *reference deck*"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information · University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Deck setup. Runs from the project root (execute-dir: project), so the shared
# helpers import the same way from any module depth.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "shared")

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import slide_helpers as sh

sh.use_slide_style()

# >>> PLACEHOLDER — replace with the deployed least-squares explorer URL. <<<
# Single source of truth for the live-demo iframe AND its fallback caption.
PLACEHOLDER_URL = "https://example.org/REPLACE-ME-least-squares-explorer"
```

## Learning outcomes {.smaller}

By the end of this module you should be able to:

1. **Placeholder outcome** — state the linear model and the squared-error loss
   $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w})$ in PML notation.
2. **Placeholder outcome** — derive the normal equations and solve for
   $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$.
3. **Placeholder outcome** — fit a line to real data and read the result
   critically (units, leverage, what the slope means).

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: these three bullets are placeholders — Greg swaps in the real
outcomes. Keep them to three, verb-first, and aligned to the checkpoint.
:::

# The least-squares problem {.center}

*A section divider — set up the geometry before the algebra.*

## The normal equations

We model targets as a linear function of the inputs and minimize squared error:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{N}\,\lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2 .
$$

Setting $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} = \mathbf{0}$ gives the **normal equations**
$\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\,\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}$, so when
$\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ is invertible,

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}\;}
$$

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: in practice we solve the linear system rather than form the
inverse — `np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ y)`. Mention conditioning here.
:::

## A least-squares fit to NHANES

```{python}
#| label: fig-ls-fit
#| fig-alt: "Scatter of systolic blood pressure against age for ~5100 NHANES adults (blue points), with an upward-sloping orange least-squares fit line showing systolic BP rising with age."
#| fig-cap: "Systolic BP vs. age, NHANES 2021–2022, with the least-squares line."

ds   = sh.load_nhanes()
age  = sh.primary_predictor(ds)      # 1-D primary predictor
sbp  = ds["y"]                       # continuous target (systolic BP)

# Solve the normal equations from scratch (instructor code; the loader ships no
# estimators — fitting is the student's job).
X    = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(age), age])
w    = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ sbp)
grid = np.linspace(age.min(), age.max(), 200)
yhat = w[0] + w[1] * grid

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(age, sbp, s=10, alpha=0.20,
           color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"], label="NHANES adults")
ax.plot(grid, yhat, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], linestyle="-",
        label=fr"$\hat y = {w[0]:.0f} + {w[1]:.2f}\,\mathrm{{age}}$")
ax.set_xlabel("age (years)")
ax.set_ylabel("systolic BP (mmHg)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Scatter of systolic BP vs age with a least-squares fit line.")
plt.show()
```

## Live demo: least-squares explorer

```{python}
#| label: live-iframe
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import HTML
HTML(
    f'<iframe src="{PLACEHOLDER_URL}" title="Least-squares explorer" '
    'width="100%" height="460" loading="lazy" '
    'style="border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:8px; background:var(--surface);">'
    '</iframe>'
)
```

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: drag the points and watch $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ update. Replace
`PLACEHOLDER_URL` (top of deck) with the deployed explorer once it ships.
:::

## Explorer — static fallback {.smaller}

*The iframe above does not print or export to PDF; this snapshot stands in.*

```{python}
#| label: fig-fallback
#| fig-alt: "Static snapshot standing in for the interactive explorer: the same NHANES systolic-BP-versus-age scatter and least-squares line, captioned as a fallback for print and PDF."
#| fig-cap: "Static fallback snapshot of the explorer (used in print / PDF)."

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(age, sbp, s=10, alpha=0.20,
           color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"], label="NHANES adults")
ax.plot(grid, yhat, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], label="least-squares fit")
ax.set_xlabel("age (years)")
ax.set_ylabel("systolic BP (mmHg)")
ax.set_title("explorer snapshot (placeholder)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Static fallback snapshot of the least-squares explorer.")
plt.show()
```

## Checkpoint preview

Next, in the peer-engagement loop you will:

- **Placeholder** — fit the line yourself in NumPy and report $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$.
- **Placeholder** — interpret the slope in clinical units (mmHg per year).
- **Placeholder** — reflect, peer-review, and revise toward the mastery token.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: this slide bridges to the activities repo
(`week01-least-squares`). Keep the verbs parallel with its quiz objectives.
:::
